title: School Guardian Act of 2025
plain_summary: The School Guardian Act of 2025 wants to put armed police officers in every elementary and high school in America. The federal government would give money to states, and then states would give that money to local police departments. The police departments would have to promise to hire at least one full-time armed officer for each school in their area. This bill is trying to make schools safer by having police officers there all the time.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the School Guardian Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 School Guardian Grants
sections.text: This section adds a new part to an existing crime control law from nineteen sixty eight. The new part creates a grant program called School Guardian Grants. Under this program, the Attorney General will give money to states that choose to participate. States can use this money to pay for armed police officers to work at elementary and high schools. The money can pay for the officers' salaries, training, and equipment. Each state gets money based on how many students attend schools in that state compared to all students in the country. The head of each state's main police agency will manage how the money gets used. States can give smaller grants to local police departments. These local police departments must make written agreements with schools in their area. The agreements must promise to hire at least one full time police officer for each school. The amount of money a local police department gets depends on how many officers they promise to hire. Each state must write a report each year about how they used the money. The report must include how many smaller grants they gave out, how much money each smaller grant was worth, how many police officers they hired for schools, and how many schools now have full time officers paid for with this money. If a state does not use all their money by the end of the year, they must give the leftover money back to the Attorney General. The Attorney General can then use that returned money for grants to other states. The Attorney General must tell Congress each year how much money was returned by each state. To pay for this program, eighty billion dollars will be taken from money that was supposed to go to the Internal Revenue Service under a law passed in two thousand twenty two. The Attorney General can give out up to eight billion dollars in grants each year from two thousand twenty five through two thousand thirty four.
